---
title: "Delta Hedging a Vanilla Option"
date: "2026-06-18"
categories: [greeks-hedging]
description: "An introduction to delta hedging and how it eliminates first-order directional risk."
image: thumbnail.png
bibliography: references.bib
nocite: '@*'
---

Delta hedging is the most fundamental risk management technique in options trading. By continuously adjusting a position in the underlying, a trader can eliminate first-order exposure to price movements.

## What is Delta?

The delta $\Delta$ of an option measures the sensitivity of the option's price to a small change in the underlying asset price:

$$\Delta = \frac{\partial V}{\partial S}$$

For a European call under Black-Scholes, $\Delta = N(d_1)$, where $N(\cdot)$ is the standard normal CDF.

## Constructing the Hedge

To delta hedge a short call position, a trader buys $\Delta$ shares of the underlying. As $S$ moves, $\Delta$ changes — so the hedge must be rebalanced continuously (in theory) or at discrete intervals (in practice).

## The Cost of Hedging

Rebalancing is not free. The P&L of a delta-hedged position depends on the difference between realised volatility and implied volatility — a key insight that connects hedging to volatility trading.

## References

::: {#refs}
:::

<hr class="revision-divider">
<details>
<summary>Revision History</summary>

- June 2026 — First published

</details>
